explanation:
The natural language requirement states that the system must perform two actions for breathing rate: measuring and displaying. These will be mapped to the atomic propositions measureRR (measuring the breathing rate) and displayRR (displaying the breathing rate). Since the requirement is a persistent one ('shall measure and display'), the correct property should require that both propositions (measureRR and displayRR) always eventually hold—meaning that the system will repeatedly measure and display the breathing rate. Using the LTL formula G (measureRR & displayRR) indicates that at every moment, both measuring and displaying are happening simultaneously. 

Alternative formulations using F (eventually) alone would be too weak, as they might allow for the system to measure and display only once. Using G (measureRR) & G (displayRR) is equivalent here to G (measureRR & displayRR). Thus, G (measureRR & displayRR) is the most direct and correct mapping for this persistent requirement.

The natural language requirement specifies that the system must both measure and display the breathing rate. The atomic propositions relevant to this are 'measureRR' (system measures the breathing rate) and 'displayRR' (system displays the breathing rate). The requirement does not specify any ordering or condition, only that both actions must happen. A standard way to specify that both eventually occur is with an 'F' (eventually) operator combined with logical AND, 'F(measureRR) & F(displayRR)'. However, because 'shall' often indicates an ongoing capability, a stronger LTL property is preferable: that globally, if the system is in operation, it should always measure and display the rate. Since there is no explicit proposition for 'system in operation', the best we can do is: globally, both 'measureRR' and 'displayRR' eventually continue to occur (i.e., G(F(measureRR) & F(displayRR))). This states that at any point in time, it will always be true that both measuring and displaying the breathing rate can eventually (i.e., in the future) occur, reflecting that these functions are persistently available and not just one-time events.

The natural language states that the system shall both measure and display the breathing rate. We interpret this as requiring that these actions always happen in the system (not necessarily in lock-step, but always satisfied). 

Given the available atomic propositions (APs): 'measureRR' refers to measurement of breathing rate and 'displayRR' refers to display of breathing rate. Since these are the only relevant APs directly reflecting the NL requirement, a globally (G) operator is used to specify that it should always be true that the system measures and displays RR.

We use conjunction (AND) to require both measurement and display. If previous LTLs only required or or used implication, that would not fulfill the 'shall measure and display' requirement: both must occur.

Thus, the correct LTL property is: G (measureRR & displayRR)

The input natural language requirement is: 'The system shall measure and display the breathing rate (number of breathes per minute).' Of the atomic propositions provided, 'measureRR' (system measures the breathing rate) and 'displayRR' (system displays the breathing rate) are the two that directly correspond to the specification. To fulfill the requirement, both actions must occur. In LTL, expressing that both 'measureRR' and 'displayRR' should eventually happen (at least once) could be written as F(measureRR & displayRR), but since the requirement implies continuous operation (as long as the system runs, it should always measure and display the breathing rate), the strongest and most accurate form is that globally, whenever the system is operating, both measuring and displaying the breathing rate should always occur. In absence of a single 'operating' atomic proposition, we interpret the statement in a safety-liveness style: 'G(measureRR & displayRR)' – this asserts that it is always the case that both measuring and displaying the breathing rate are active, covering the intended meaning of the requirement. This translation is more comprehensive than simply F(measureRR) & F(displayRR), which would only require each to occur at least once, not continuously, thus possibly missing the requirement’s intent of ongoing capability.

The input natural language specification states that the system must measure and display the breathing rate. According to the provided atomic propositions, 'measureRR' indicates measurement of the breathing rate and 'displayRR' indicates displaying the breathing rate. The requirement can be captured in LTL by specifying that globally (always), if the system measures the breathing rate, it must also display the breathing rate. This ensures that whenever the measurement happens, the display follows, fulfilling the spirit of the requirement for both actions to occur together. A simpler (but still strong) interpretation would require both actions always eventually to happen, but the direct causal connection is most faithful to the natural language. The formulas do not employ any numerical comparison operators, as required.

The natural language requirement is that the system must both measure and display the breathing rate (breaths per minute). Based on the provided atomic propositions, 'measureRR' indicates that the system measures the breathing rate and 'displayRR' indicates that the system displays the breathing rate. To capture the requirement, we state that it is always (globally) required that when the system measures the breathing rate, it also displays it at the same time. This is best encoded as G(measureRR -> displayRR) and additionally to ensure both are eventually achieved, we say G(F measureRR & F displayRR), though the first property (the implication) directly covers the coordination requirement. However, since the atomic propositions do not distinguish between different 'breathes per minute' values (i.e., no parameterization), and only refer to the act of measuring or displaying the rate, G(measureRR -> displayRR) best captures the requirement, as it states that whenever breathing rate is measured, it is also displayed. If either is not present, the implication would not be triggered, which matches the intent. Other formulations may not guarantee synchronicity or the 'whenever measured, must display' constraint.

The input states that the system should both measure and display the breathing rate. According to the atomic propositions, 'measureRR' represents measurement of the breathing rate, and 'displayRR' represents that the system displays the breathing rate. To capture the requirement that both actions should continually (globally) happen (i.e., the system always measures and displays the breathing rate at all times), we use the LTL formula G(measureRR & displayRR). This formula ensures that at all points in time, both measuring and displaying the breathing rate are true. No alternative LTL expressions using the given atomic propositions would better capture the 'and' requirement for these simultaneous properties. Previous possible (but incorrect) LTL expressions could have used only measurement or only display, or could have used 'eventually' (F) instead of 'globally' (G), which would not fully capture the requirement that these properties must hold at all times.

The requirement 'The system shall measure and display the breathing rate (number of breathes per minute)' indicates that whenever the system measures the breathing rate, it should also display it, and both should continually be performed throughout operation. Using the atomic propositions, this is captured by stating that it is always the case (globally, G) that if the system measures the breathing rate (measureRR), then it also displays the breathing rate (displayRR), and vice versa. Thus: G (measureRR <-> displayRR). There are no atomic propositions for specific values or for a minute nor direct triggers, so this abstraction best matches the intent using only the available atomic propositions. Previous LTLs might try to separate these activities, but the mutual implication ensures these occur together whenever the system operates.

The input specifies that the system must both measure and display the breathing rate. These tasks correspond to the atomic propositions 'measureRR' (indicates the system measures the breathing rate) and 'displayRR' (indicates the system displays the breathing rate). To express that the system shall always (globally) do both tasks, we use the 'globally' (G) operator in LTL. The combined property G(measureRR & displayRR) states that it is always the case that the system is measuring and displaying the breathing rate. This is the best possible translation using the available propositions, as neither refers strictly to 'per minute' or the calculation itself, but together they capture the essential stated requirement in the absence of a 'calculate breathing rate' atomic proposition.

The input natural language states that the system shall measure and display the breathing rate (number of breaths per minute). Among the provided atomic propositions, 'measureRR' indicates the system measures the breathing rate, and 'displayRR' indicates the system displays the breathing rate. The requirement implies that both measuring and displaying should always happen. The LTL property 'G(measureRR & displayRR)' expresses that globally, at all times, both the measurement and the display of the breathing rate will hold, which matches the intent of the input requirement. Alternatives such as only using 'F' (eventually) or only 'measureRR' or 'displayRR' individually would not guarantee the continuous behavior required by 'shall measure and display'.
